comments.comment_body: This isn’t SaaS + AI. It’s a new class of software altogether. Nailed it.
comments.post_body:
"SaaS is dead in 2025."
Everyone said it. Were they right?

Sort of.
Legacy SaaS is dying. If you're building another project management tool, CRM, or sales engagement platform, good luck. The market is saturated. Buyers are done paying for seats they don't need.

But here's what the doomers got wrong:
AI isn't killing software. It's birthing an entirely new species.

We're not talking about ChatGPT wrappers or "AI features." We're talking about:
→ Buyer agents that research vendors, negotiate, and purchase without you
→ Autonomous transaction systems that make real-time decisions at scale
→ Workflow infrastructure that replaces entire teams, not just tasks

This isn't "SaaS + AI."
It's something we don't even have a name for yet.

And the business models? They're getting rewritten too. Per-seat pricing made sense when software was a tool. What's the pricing model when software is the employee?
We're figuring it out in real time.

So no, SaaS isn't dead.
But what it becomes next? That's wide open.
comments.context_summary: The post discusses how legacy SaaS is dying due to a saturated market, but argues that AI is not killing software, instead giving birth to a new species of autonomous systems that will replace entire teams and rewrite business models. The commenter is responding to the idea that SaaS is dead in 2025, and presents a vision of a future where software becomes an integral part of business operations.

comments.comment_body: Iffat Hafsa Absolutely. These shifts have been disruptive. At the same time, I’m already seeing many people come back stronger as they adapt and work with AI, which really reinforces why staying in touch with technology matters.
comments.post_body:
Technology unlocks new possibilities.

Suddenly, the same opportunity becomes visible to many people.

Some stay ideas.
Some get built.

The difference usually shows up in what gets tested.
comments.context_summary: The post discusses how technology creates new opportunities that become visible to many, and the commenter is responding to the idea that some ideas get built and tested while others do not. The conversation is centered around the role of technology in turning ideas into reality.

comments.comment_body:
Fully agree.
SaaS will just absorb AI by adding agentic layers so users can access features via prompts instead of complex workflows.
comments.post_body:
"SaaS is dead".
I don't believe it.

January isn’t even over, and the feed is already full of confident predictions:

✔️  SaaS is dying
✔️  AI agents will replace platforms
✔️  Web coding + no-code = the end of software

I skipped public year-end summaries this year. But this narrative is too loud to ignore.

SaaS isn’t disappearing.
It’s mutating.

Yes, AI agents change how we interact with products. Yes, building tools is becoming easier.

But the idea that businesses will suddenly build and run their own infrastructure - even with AI - is detached from reality.

Because infrastructure is still:
1️⃣ expensive
2️⃣ complex
3️⃣ risky to scale

And owning it is rarely a competitive advantage. Most companies don’t want more things to build. They want systems that just work, integrate fast, and adapt to their workflows.

The future looks like this:

– SaaS becomes AI-native
– Products turn into intelligent layers, not rigid platforms
– AI agents don’t replace SaaS - they operate inside it

So no - SaaS isn’t dying. Bad, inflexible SaaS is.

💡 What do you think:

Will AI replace SaaS - or will SaaS quietly absorb it and evolve?
comments.context_summary: The commenter is responding to the post 'SaaS is dead' and disagrees, arguing that SaaS is not dying but rather evolving to become AI-native, with AI agents operating inside SaaS products to make them more intelligent and adaptable. The commenter believes that bad, inflexible SaaS will be replaced, not SaaS as a whole.

comments.comment_body:
I’m doubling down on checker skills, catching bugs, missing constraints, and edge cases AI introduces due to incomplete prompts.
 AI can write code; engineers must protect outcomes.
comments.post_body:
AI stopped being a cute copilot

1/ Top engineers report AI agents now write most of their production code, across web, backend, and even C.​
2/ New models (Opus 4.5, GPT‑5.2, Gemini 3) crossed an invisible line: suddenly, complex tasks that used to be painful are now “one‑shot” solvable.​

🧠 The bad news for “just coders”

1/ Being “the person who types fast in language X” is rapidly losing value; AI can jump stacks better than most humans.​
2/ Tasks like prototyping, refactoring, and implementing well‑defined tickets will be increasingly delegated to AI.​

🛠 The good news for engineers

1/ Clarifying what to build: breaking down vague ideas into precise behavior, edge cases, and constraints.​
2/ Owning quality: testing, CI/CD, observability, reliability, performance, security, tech debt.​
3/ Thinking like a product engineer: close to users, shipping fast, deciding what matters vs noise.​

AI writes more lines; humans own the outcomes.

😬 The ugly side

1/ More code → more bugs, more complexity, more infra cost; change failure rates are already going up in some orgs.​
2/ Mobile AI coding means “coding from your phone” becomes normal, and work–life boundaries risk being erased even more.​

💼 What this means for careers

1/ Juniors will be pushed to think like seniors earlier: architecture, testing, trade‑offs, not just syntax.​
2/ A solid CS / software engineering foundation may become a minimum bar, not a nice‑to‑have.​
3/ Demand for good software engineers might actually rise, as the amount of AI‑generated software explodes.​

Are you already letting AI write 50–90% of your code?
What skills are you actively doubling down on for the next 5 years?
#AI #SoftwareEngineering #DeveloperExperience #LLM #ProductEngineering
comments.context_summary: The post discusses how AI is increasingly writing most of the production code, changing the role of engineers and the skills required to be successful in the field, with a focus on high-level tasks like clarifying what to build and owning quality. The commenter is responding to the post's exploration of the impact of AI on software engineering and the need for engineers to adapt and develop new skills.

comments.comment_body: Alain V. Tools speed up coding. Teams deliver outcomes. We position ourselves as the execution team that leverages these tools to ship things reliably.
comments.post_body:
Over time, I’ve noticed a simple pattern.
Most founders don’t struggle with ideas.

They struggle with taking things from idea to live.

I’m building CodingRippler to help founders ship products, not decks, not endless POCs, and not “almost ready” systems.

In practice, that usually means:

→ Turning ideas or early concepts into live products
→ Rebuilding fragile systems so teams can move faster
→ Automating workflows that quietly slow the business down

We work as an extension of the founder’s team.
Senior execution. Clear ownership. No disappearing.

If you’re building something and want to take it live, or get an existing product unstuck, happy to exchange notes.

No pitch. Just builders talking to builders.
comments.context_summary: The post discusses the challenges founders face in turning ideas into live products and introduces CodingRippler as a solution to help them ship products efficiently. The commenter is responding to the post by offering to exchange notes with fellow builders who want to take their products live or get unstuck.

comments.comment_body: Really appreciate this way of thinking.
comments.post_body:
i went from charging $397 to $4,658/mo in 4 months.

same product.
nothing changed.
just charged 11 times more.
actually, it's less work now (rich people are less needy).

people always say "sell to the rich" but... 
nobody tells you HOW to actually do that.

so here's how i did it.

1. be more specific—use industry lingo.

you HAVE to talk like them.
non-negotiable.
the $397 client? i told them i could get them "lEaDs."
the $4,658 client? it was "strategic partnerships with R&D directors."

same thing.
different language.
stop saying "i can get you leads."
say "i can connect you with CFOs at mid-market PE firms."
or "i can book meetings with procurement directors at Fortune 500s."
industry lingo = you're speaking their language = premium pricing.

2. make deliverables LESS complicated.

the $397 client had to:

- sign up for 3 different softwares
- create logins
- join a slack workspace
- check a custom dashboard

the $4,658 client?

- i send them a text every 2 weeks.
- and we share a google sheet.

that's it.

no bullshit.
they pay premium to NOT deal with complications.
so i deal with it all.

3. find blue ocean markets—bring tech into OLD industries.

stop targeting mArkETing aGENciEs.
go after OLD MONEY.

wealth management.
biotech.
logistics.
investment banking.

these industries are still doing things the old way.

if you bring "modern" tech, you can charge a shit ton for simple stuff.

the $397 client was an accounting firm selling $5K packages.
the $4,658 client was biotech selling $6M contracts.

see the difference?

4. work with boomers.

i love working with boomers.
they're more patient.
they're blown away by the simplest advancements.
they're used to things taking weeks.

younger entrepreneurs expect FAST results.
boomers don't.

the $397 client was in his 30s.
the $4,658 client was in his 60s.

and guess who was easier to work with?

(the $397 client was still a pleasure to work with.)

ps. how much do you charge for your products?
comments.context_summary: The commenter shares their experience of increasing their monthly charge from $397 to $4,658 in 4 months by targeting high-end clients and adjusting their approach to use industry lingo, simplify deliverables, and find niche markets. They provide specific tips on how to achieve premium pricing, including working with older, more patient clients who are willing to pay for simplicity and expertise.

comments.comment_body: Same realization here. The hype said agents would replace teams, but in reality AI works best when it supports people.
comments.post_body:
"𝐀𝐈 𝐚𝐠𝐞𝐧𝐭𝐬 𝐰𝐞𝐫𝐞 𝐬𝐮𝐩𝐩𝐨𝐬𝐞𝐝 𝐭𝐨 𝐭𝐚𝐤𝐞 𝐨𝐯𝐞𝐫 𝐢𝐧 2025. 𝐈𝐧𝐬𝐭𝐞𝐚𝐝, 42% 𝐨𝐟 𝐜𝐨𝐦𝐩𝐚𝐧𝐢𝐞𝐬 𝐣𝐮𝐬𝐭 𝐚𝐛𝐚𝐧𝐝𝐨𝐧𝐞𝐝 𝐭𝐡𝐞𝐢𝐫𝐬."

Since November, companies that rushed to deploy AI agents are quietly pulling back. Projects are stalling. Budgets are being questioned. And hardly anyone's talking about it publicly.

While tech giants were celebrating their agent launches in November -OpenAI's Agent Mode, Perplexity's Comet Browser, Google's Gemini 3 - the reality in production environments tells a different story. Deloitte's Tech Trends 2025 report reveals that only 11% of organizations currently use AI agents in production, despite intense hype.

𝐇𝐞𝐫𝐞'𝐬 𝐰𝐡𝐚𝐭'𝐬 𝐚𝐜𝐭𝐮𝐚𝐥𝐥𝐲 𝐛𝐫𝐞𝐚𝐤𝐢𝐧𝐠 𝐭𝐡𝐞𝐦:

Agentic AI systems already support scientific discovery, software development, and clinical research, yet they still struggle with unreliable tool use, weak long-horizon planning, and poor generalization. A Stanford and Harvard research paper published in December finally explains why agents feel impressive in demos, then completely fall apart in real use.

𝐓𝐡𝐞 𝐮𝐧𝐜𝐨𝐦𝐟𝐨𝐫𝐭𝐚𝐛𝐥𝐞 𝐝𝐚𝐭𝐚:

In November, a study found that agents powered by top LLMs from OpenAI, Google DeepMind, and Anthropic failed to complete many straightforward workplace tasks by themselves. But when the same agents worked alongside humans who knew what they were doing, success rates shot up dramatically.

Sam Altman announced a 'code red' for ChatGPT in December, asking his teams to refocus away from agents and get back to improving the core ChatGPT user experience. That tells you everything about where we actually are versus where the hype said we'd be.

𝐖𝐡𝐚𝐭'𝐬 𝐚𝐜𝐭𝐮𝐚𝐥𝐥𝐲 𝐰𝐨𝐫𝐤𝐢𝐧𝐠:

The pattern is clear - agents aren't replacing humans, they're augmenting experts. The organizations succeeding aren't deploying autonomous agents; they're building human-AI collaboration systems where judgment-heavy tasks stay with people.
comments.context_summary: The commenter is responding to a post about the hype surrounding AI agents and how they are not living up to expectations, with many companies quietly pulling back on their deployment and instead focusing on human-AI collaboration systems. The post highlights the limitations of AI agents, including their struggle with unreliable tool use and poor generalization, and how they are more effective when working alongside humans.

comments.comment_body: Totally agree, Harsh Kumar — AI really shines only in the hands of experienced people.

comments.comment_body: True to every word.
comments.post_body:
🔥 “I didn’t write a single line of code this month.”

Boris Cherny, engineer 👨‍💻  at Anthropic and the creator of Claude Code, an AI-powered coding tool associated with the Claude family of model, recently tweeted such sentence. 

👉 What’s now a core developer tool started as a side project.
That’s amazing - and very telling.

IMHO with tools like Claude Code, Copilot, Cursor, etc., typing code is no longer the bottleneck.

 What’s becoming scarce is something else:
 • Clear intent
 • Good architecture
 • Strong constraints
 • High-quality review
 • Ownership of outcomes

When an AI “writes” the code, the engineer doesn’t disappear - they move up the stack.
You still:
▫️ decide what should exist
▫️ define how it should behave
▫️ review, reject, refine, and secure it
▫️ ensure it fits the product, the system, and reality

Saying “the AI wrote 100% of the code” is like saying a pilot didn’t fly because the autopilot was on. True in a narrow sense. Misleading in the one that matters.
The real change isn’t fewer engineers.
It’s engineers becoming directors, editors, and quality owners instead of typists. And this is still the beginning.
#SoftwareEngineering #AI #DevTools #ProductThinking #FutureOfWork #Claude #LLMs
comments.context_summary: The commenter is responding to a post about Boris Cherny, an engineer who tweeted that he didn't write a single line of code in a month, and discusses how AI-powered coding tools are changing the role of engineers from typists to directors and quality owners. The commenter argues that with tools like Claude Code, the bottleneck in software development is no longer typing code, but rather clear intent, good architecture, and high-quality review.

comments.comment_body: Agreed.
comments.post_body:
Your biggest barrier to AI agent success is memory. Here’s what I’ve learned about giving AI the right context (after building multiple 7-figure ROI-generating AI solutions):

The biggest mistake enterprises make with AI is not starting.

After that, the biggest mistake is throwing all their data and context at one model, and expecting it to figure everything out.

It won’t.

1. Performance gets worse as you throw more information at a context window

2. Like humans, AI agents start to break down as you give them more varied tasks

3. Data’s often in different sizes and types, meaning you can’t apply one chunking strategy to everything equally

This poster is right - this is why long-term memory is the biggest limitation to agents.

But there’s a few things you can do to make agentic systems actually work with lots of data.

1. Distribute the duties. Instead of one agent to handle everything, break into specialized sub-agents to handle specific tasks in the workflow.

2. On system entry and before system exit, add fact-checking and security steps to make sure you’re getting the output you’re expecting, and large context windows aren’t causing the agent to lose control.

3. Give the agent as little to do as possible. Many systems we design only have AI for a small component, and use deterministic automations for the rest.

Does this solve the memory problem? Of course not. Not by itself.

Does it create AI solutions that generate real ROI for our customers? Of course it does.

That’s the difference.
comments.context_summary: The commenter agrees that memory is a significant barrier to AI agent success and shares their experience with building successful AI solutions, highlighting the importance of distributing duties, fact-checking, and limiting agent tasks to improve performance. They emphasize that while these strategies don't completely solve the memory problem, they can lead to AI solutions that generate real ROI for customers.

comments.comment_body: Congratulations on the success of Phase One! Best wishes to Eli and look forward to hearing about your next venture.
comments.post_body:
Today’s my last day as CEO at Automation Helpers.

What’s coming next is even bigger.

When Eli and I started Automation Helpers three years ago, our goal was simple: help small businesses operationalize and automate their backend processes.

Phase One was all about learning: building a services agency that could get close to the real challenges small businesses face. That phase has been a huge success: we’ve worked with hundreds of customers and grown to over $2M in annual revenue.

Now it’s time for Phase Two.

To take Automation Helpers from $2M to $5M and beyond, I’m thrilled to share that Eli Geske will be stepping into the CEO role. Eli’s leadership and operational focus make him the perfect person to lead this next chapter of growth.

As for me, I’ll still be involved in an advisory role, but I’ll be stepping out of day-to-day operations to focus on what’s next: building something new that extends the same mission we started with, in a whole new way.

More to come soon. 🚀
comments.context_summary: The CEO of Automation Helpers is stepping down and passing the role to Eli Geske, as the company enters its next phase of growth, with the outgoing CEO moving into an advisory role and planning to build something new. The company has achieved $2M in annual revenue and aims to reach $5M and beyond under new leadership.

comments.comment_body: wow
comments.post_body:
I've built 50+ AI agents and every single one started with this simple blueprint...

Most people jump straight into building and wonder why their agent doesn't work.

The Agent Blueprint fixes this in 10 minutes:

- Forces you to define the actual problem (not just "automate stuff")
- Maps out exactly when and how it runs
- Identifies what data it needs access to
- Breaks the logic into clear steps
- Specifies where outputs should go

Once you fill it out, you can just describe it in plain English to an AI tool that builds the entire agent for you.

Perfect if you're non-technical.

No connecting nodes, no dragging boxes.

Just chat your way to a working agent.

The blueprint includes:

- The 5-question framework
- Full live tutorial showing me go from idea to working agent in under 10 minutes
- Cheat sheet with common trigger types, action verbs, data sources, output destinations
- 5 quick patterns you can steal

Connect with me + comment "AGENT" and I'll DM you the blueprint.
comments.context_summary: The commenter is responding to a post about a simple blueprint for building AI agents, which helps define the problem, map out the agent's logic, and identify necessary data, allowing for easy creation of a working agent even for non-technical individuals. The post offers a free blueprint to those who connect with the author and comment 'AGENT'.

comments.comment_body: Absolutely, Oleksandra Bielova , I completely agree. Many teams take up automation just to show progress, even when it’s not really needed. Often it’s more about keeping leadership happy than solving a real problem.
comments.post_body:
We’re seeing this a lot in the automation space, and honestly, 𝐈’𝐯𝐞 𝐛𝐞𝐞𝐧 𝐠𝐮𝐢𝐥𝐭𝐲 𝐨𝐟 𝐢𝐭 𝐭𝐨𝐨.

Most automation agencies make one mistake, they try to sell automation.
But selling automation often adds cost before it adds value.

Because in the early stages, most of what automation promises can actually be done manually, faster, cheaper, and with more context.

The real need for automation begins when manual work becomes the bottleneck, when scaling becomes painful, repetitive, and error-prone.

That’s when automation stops being a “nice-to-have” and becomes an enabler of growth.

So instead of trying to sell automation, we build it where the pain is real, where teams are already doing the work, and scaling it manually no longer makes sense.

At CodingRippler, that’s how we think about automation,
not as a product to push, but as a capability that emerges naturally when scaling requires it.

Maybe automation shouldn’t be sold, it should be discovered.

📅 Curious if your team has reached the point where automation actually makes sense?
Let’s explore that together → [https://lnkd.in/d2CPjnDE]

📖 Want to see how other teams identified the right moments to automate and what impact it created?
Explore our case studies → [https://lnkd.in/dekJMwPd]
comments.context_summary: The post discusses the common mistake automation agencies make by trying to sell automation, and instead suggests that automation should be built where the pain is real and scaling manually no longer makes sense, enabling growth when manual work becomes a bottleneck. The commenter is responding to this idea, potentially considering how it applies to their own team's situation.

comments.comment_body: Nice one! Always up to exchange ideas.
comments.post_body:
Here’s how I built an AI-powered inbox classifier using n8n

Step-by-step:
1️⃣ Trigger runs automatically whenever a new email is received
2️⃣ The workflow uses a custom text classifier trained on your labeled inboxes (e.g., Priority, Finance, Promotion, Clients, Internal)
3️⃣ It determines which category the email belongs to
4️⃣ Then it labels, routes, and even drafts a reply based on the context

This AI Inbox Classifier saves business owners hours every week sorting through endless messages — instantly flagging what’s important, and automating replies to routine emails.

You can easily connect it with Gmail, Slack, Notion, or any CRM, and have your AI assistant manage incoming messages for you in real time.

Quick question:

If you were to automate ONE part of your inbox, what would it be?
• support
• sales
• internal
• something else?

Comment "n8n" and I’ll send the workflow.
#n8n #AIautomation #EmailAutomation #WorkflowAutomation #Productivity #NoCode #Gmail #AIagents
comments.context_summary: The commenter is responding to a post about building an AI-powered inbox classifier using n8n, which automates email sorting and replies, and is being asked to share which part of their inbox they would automate if they could choose one thing. The post author is offering to share their n8n workflow with those who comment 'n8n'.

comments.comment_body: My vote goes for AI Launch Plan
comments.post_body:
I'm naming my AI GTM plan builder and need your vote.

After digging into search data, "launch" terminology crushes "GTM" for SMB discovery. Founders search for "launch plans" 3-5x more than GTM frameworks.

So I've narrowed it to three names:

1️⃣ AI Launch Plan (lead candidate)
Most direct, matches exact search intent

2️⃣ AI Launch Blueprint
Premium feel, maps to strategy searches

3️⃣ AI Launch Deck
Ties to the 40-page deliverable format

Which name would make you click? Which one actually sounds like something you'd use?

Drop your vote in the comments (just say 1️⃣, 2️⃣, or 3️⃣). And if you hate all of them, tell me that too.

Since I'm building this in public I want to get more feedback before I lock in domains and publish the landing page.

Thanks!

comments.comment_body: AI isn’t about disappearing people, it’s about making them superstars at what they do best!
comments.post_body:
Want to see an AI magic trick?


Many big companies are rushing to perfect the 'make employees disappear' trick. But I think they're completely missing the mark.

When Voice AI can handle half of all frontline banking interactions, leaders face a fascinating choice: reduce their workforce, or redirect that freed-up human potential into deeper customer relationships and revenue growth.

Let's fast forward to 2035. Which approach builds a more loyal customer community?

I get it—when banking leaders first explore AI, immediate cost reduction is appealing. It’s a bit of an ‘abracadabra’ moment. With today's pressures, who wouldn't consider it? 

But when we dig into their core strategic goals—sustainable growth, market resilience, competitive edge—the conversation shifts. They begin to see AI not just as a replacement strategy, but as a way to recalibrate their teams for growth AND cost savings.

The organizations that truly get it? They're using AI to achieve what was previously impossible with their frontline teams. That's where the real magic happens. No tricks, no illusions—just the transformative power of human potential amplified by AI.

Apologies in advance to the magic community if my use of abracadabra isn’t appropriate in this context. 🤣
comments.context_summary: The post discusses how big companies are using AI to automate tasks, but instead of just reducing their workforce, they should redirect the freed-up human potential into deeper customer relationships and revenue growth, achieving sustainable growth and a competitive edge. The commenter argues that AI can amplify human potential, leading to transformative power and growth.

comments.comment_body: Adding your own touch makes all the difference
comments.post_body:
Just a quick reminder: if you’re sending an email, writing an article, or posting on socials, don’t copy and paste from ChatGPT or any AI.

I skip anything that reads like a paste. It gives me the impression of dealing with zero initiative, zero creativity, and zero willingness to add value or actually engage.

Use AI (I do, every day). But, please, don’t make your writing generic and soulless. 

We will all get tired of that soon and start questioning the desire to really work or collaborate.

Bring your voice. Edit. Add context. Make it yours.

#ai
comments.context_summary: The commenter is emphasizing the importance of originality and creativity in writing, discouraging the practice of copying and pasting from AI tools like ChatGPT, and encouraging individuals to add their own voice and context to their work. This is in response to a post warning against using AI-generated content without editing or adding personal touches.

comments.comment_body: Great points! Building AI responsibly from the start creates trust and long-term value
comments.post_body:
We are entering a world where the power of AI needs to be checked and ensured that it’s compliant to our values. I see product managers miss this aspect a lot — here is a good read/case study on how to go about it.
 
1. Embed Ethical & Value Checks Early — and Continuously: Microsoft established that Azure cannot be used for surveillance and ensured checks in monitoring and auditing. 

2. Be Transparent About Criteria & Trade-offs: Beyond azure auditing systems, this meant they needed a third-party law firm to investigate and prove this was happening. Also, they fired everyone who broke their employee code of conduct and created noise without facts earlier on it.

3. Align Incentives to Long-Term Value: This means they are doing the hard things here — losing money on the table, justifying their stand under very tight scrutiny that will follow, but in the end it will create long-term value.

https://lnkd.in/guYt-Nwe
comments.context_summary: The post discusses the importance of ensuring AI compliance with human values, sharing a case study on Microsoft's approach to embedding ethical checks and transparency in their Azure product. The commenter is responding to the post by presumably agreeing with or adding to the discussion on AI ethics and compliance.

comments.comment_body: AI is a great tool, but if we stop thinking ourselves, it will end up replacing us
comments.post_body:
I have started asking candidates, "Can you do this without AI?"
Seems like a strange thing to ask, since we are in the business of AI.
 
But what I have been observing, with alarm, is how for some folks, AI has replaced critical thinking and effort.
 
There's good research to back this. MIT found that people using ChatGPT showed 68% weaker neural connectivity. Their brains simply became less active.

That's where the biggest risk and opportunity with AI lies. 

If you want to offload thinking, it helps you become lazier.
If you want to think sharper, it becomes the best teacher. 
 
With each interaction, you make that choice. 
To become better or to become more dependent. 
 
And that is the biggest irony. 
AI will replace those who purely depend on AI.
 
#AI #CriticalThinking #FutureOfWork
comments.context_summary: The post discusses the potential risks of relying too heavily on AI, citing research that shows decreased neural connectivity in individuals who use it, and argues that AI can either hinder or enhance critical thinking depending on how it's used. The commenter is responding to the idea that AI can replace human thought and effort, and is highlighting the importance of balancing technology use with critical thinking skills.

comments.comment_body: AI doesn’t replace our work, it just helps us do it faster and smarter
comments.post_body:
🤔 Does using AI make our work worth less?

I’ve noticed a shift in how people talk about work since AI entered the picture. Just the other day, I overheard someone in a lineup say, “You don’t know what to believe anymore since AI came out.” And at work, I sometimes hear comments like, “ChatGPT must have done most of that for you.”

Those remarks stick with me, because they don’t match the reality I’ve experienced.

When I use AI, it isn’t replacing my contribution, it’s enhancing it. I still bring the knowledge, judgment, and intent. AI helps me draft faster, compile data in minutes that used to take hours, and surface insights from larger datasets than I could access on my own. But the refinement, the context, and the final decisions remain human.

I don’t think I’m alone in this. Many people are probably feeling the same unease: that using AI could make their work seem less valuable, even though in practice it makes them more effective.

This feels like part of a much larger transition society is navigating. As AI rapidly integrates into our lives, we’re still learning how to separate the tool from the talent.

Leaders have an important role here. The real opportunity isn’t just adopting AI, but recognizing and rewarding how people use it to elevate their work. If we frame AI as an amplifier of human capability, not a shortcut, we can foster trust, increase productivity, and open the door to real innovation.

The organizations that will thrive are those that see AI as a partnership between technology and human expertise, where people are empowered to do their best work while leveraging the tools that enhance it.

👉 I’d love to hear from others:
	•	Have you felt that AI use made your work seem undervalued?
	•	How should leaders help shift the perception so AI is seen as a strength rather than a weakness?
comments.context_summary: The post discusses how the increasing use of AI in work is leading to a perception that it diminishes the value of human contribution, and the author argues that AI actually enhances their work, with leaders playing a crucial role in reframing AI as a tool that amplifies human capability. The author invites others to share their experiences and thoughts on how leaders can help shift the perception of AI in the workplace.

comments.comment_body: Agentic AI takes AI to the next level by learning, adapting, and making smart decisions to boost business and customer experiences
comments.post_body:
Agentic AI: Revolutionizing Customer Experiences and Business Impact

In the realm of AI, a significant evolution is taking place. While traditional AI follows rules and generative AI focuses on content creation, Agentic AI distinguishes itself by making decisions, adapting, and driving outcomes.

What makes Agentic AI unique is its capability to:
- Learn from interactions and results
- Function within flexible, adaptive workflows
- Independently make decisions aligned with business goals
- Deliver highly personalized, context-aware experiences

No longer are we confined to generic suggestions and simple efficiency enhancements. Agentic AI operates on a whole new paradigm, actively strategizing, adapting, and implementing, transforming customer experiences and business results in unparalleled ways.

The future of AI transcends mere service—it entails forging alliances with enterprises, fostering innovation, and reshaping our understanding of AI's role in our endeavors.

#AgenticAI #AIInnovation #FutureOfWork #CustomerExperience #BusinessImpact
comments.context_summary: The post discusses Agentic AI, a revolutionary type of AI that can make decisions, adapt, and drive outcomes, transforming customer experiences and business results. The commenter is responding to the introduction of Agentic AI and its potential to forge new alliances and reshape the role of AI in business.

comments.comment_body: Jonathan Coulter Exactly! Search today is about being findable across AI, agents, and humans. That’s why we focus on clear, factual, and crawlable content that shows up wherever your audience asks
comments.post_body:
SEO isn’t just Google anymore, it’s “can assistants and agents find you?”

With a new website I'm digging into SEO and reminded just how long Google can take for visibility. 

However, in reality, a lot of buyers now ask ChatGPT/Claude/Gemini before they turn to Google and more teams are deploying AI agents (support, scheduling, sales ops) that need the same raw material to answer well.

Assistants and agent optimisation is now as important as SEO

• Make it findable, not fancy. Plain, public pages that answer real questions. FAQs, pricing ranges, locations, “who we’re for/not for.”

• Be quotable. Short, clear proof pages. Assistants love a clean source.

• Keep facts consistent everywhere. Site, LinkedIn, directories. Contradictions make assistants less confident in their recommendation.

• Write like the prompt. Use content the way people ask: “Best [service] for [problem] in [location]?” Then answer it straight.

• Document the process. Steps, SLAs, process stick when models summarise or agents explain decisions.

• Little and often. Fresh, factual updates compound faster than one perfect essay you never publish.

Does this surface faster than classic SEO?

Often yes for assistants and agents. Clear, crawlable, consistent content can be picked up in hours or days, meaningful Google movement is usually weeks or months. (Crawlability still matters for both)

Curious, has anyone had leads say, “we asked ChatGPT/Claude and your name came up” What did they see?
comments.context_summary: The commenter is discussing the evolution of SEO, highlighting the importance of optimizing for assistants and AI agents, and providing tips on how to make content findable and quotable for these new search methods. They're also asking if others have had leads generated from being discovered through ChatGPT or other AI assistants.

comments.comment_body: Totally agree! Assistants and AI agents are changing the way people find businesses

comments.comment_body: Absolutely Ben Edwards I think boutique firms can really shine here because they can give very niche, specific answers to unique problems
comments.post_body:
Many consulting firms depend on referrals... However, if you're used to generating inbound interest - how is that changing?

Are you getting any interest via LLMs like ChatGPT, Grok, Claude or Gemini (AI google search)?

If so... are you intentional about appearing when ICP prospects search for specific problems or challenges? 

It's another little area I'm thinking boutique Pro Serv firms should have the upper hand on the bigger firms on... because you should have really niche & specific answers to really niche problems & challenges... but it's just a hypothesis with no data & very little anecdotal evidence yet!
comments.context_summary: The post discusses how consulting firms can generate inbound interest, particularly through the use of large language models (LLMs) like ChatGPT, and whether boutique firms can leverage their niche expertise to gain an advantage. The commenter is exploring the potential for boutique professional services firms to capitalize on specific search queries related to their areas of expertise.

comments.comment_body:
Great post Panagiotis Spiliotopoulos! AI in pharma can really help make work faster and more accurate. I like your idea about AI agents working together like a team, it feels like the future!

At CodingRippler, we build AI tools like Sales Rep Assistants and smart automations. Would love to connect and see how we can work together on your AI projects
comments.post_body:
So lets talk about AI in pharma. 
Generative AI can be something that can improve speed and accuracy of information. Access to the necessary data is vital of course.  
E.g. a Sales Rep Assistant that can answer by retrieving the information requested thus giving a better accuracy, either for preparation, follow-up, goal achievement, etc. 
Agentic AI can be something that allows to plan and execute tasks. And of course I have seen agents assisting in creating records, while dictating or planning interactions to achieve targets, but my take is undertaking more complex tasks, like plan an event or a product launch or an informative/marketing campaign. All the latter have a more complex project approach. 
Of course, in all scenarios tools and access to data is key. An LLM is not just enough or a monolithic approach to an AI implementation, e.g. a GenAI without RAG technique (Retrieval-Augmented Generation). 
I would say that the IT eco-system has to prioritize communication between the systems, not just access but planning for an AI agent to AI agent communication.  
The future? Probably a human asking an AI agent to help with a complex task and the AI agent asks other AI agents for help. (I do not know why but I had the "Avengers Assemble" scene pop in my mind)

Final note. Humans must be in the equation to validate and have the ownership of the task/outcome. And do not be afraid to have your AI either reply “I do not know” or “I cannot assist, need clarification, do not have access, etc”. Alternative, answers and results that are not the correct.
🙂
comments.context_summary: The commenter discusses the potential of AI in the pharmaceutical industry, highlighting the importance of access to data and communication between systems for effective AI implementation, and emphasizing the need for human validation and oversight in AI-driven tasks. They envision a future where AI agents collaborate to assist humans with complex tasks, while also acknowledging the limitations and potential pitfalls of AI.

comments.comment_body: So true Kyri Chrysandreas! Too much data can create confusion instead of clarity. At CodingRippler, we help businesses focus on the right metrics by building smart dashboards and automation, so leaders can make quick, confident decisions without drowning in numbers
comments.post_body:
More data doesn’t mean better decisions.

I worked with a leadership team that had no shortage of dashboards. Every metric imaginable was available - uptime, throughput, cost per transaction, customer clicks, NPS, you name it. They had so much data that meetings turned into endless debates over which numbers actually mattered.

The result? Decisions slowed down. Teams second-guessed themselves. Opportunities passed by.

The common belief is that more data equals more clarity. In reality, more data often equals more noise. When everything is measured, nothing feels meaningful. Leaders don’t need all the data - they need the right data.

Better decisions come from context, not quantity.

• Focus on a small set of metrics tied to outcomes, not activity.
• Translate data into insights that the business can act on.
• Empower leaders with clarity, not clutter.

→ Drowning in dashboards doesn’t make you data-driven.
→ Making confident, timely decisions does.

#DataDriven #BetterDecisions #DataQuality #DataStrategy #BusinessIntelligence #DecisionMaking #DataAnalysis #InsightfulData #DataOverload #SmartDecisions
comments.context_summary: The post discusses how having too much data can lead to slower decision-making and decreased clarity, and argues that leaders should focus on a small set of meaningful metrics rather than trying to analyze everything. The commenter is responding to the idea that more data does not necessarily equal better decisions.

comments.comment_body:
Totally agree! Ojassvi Pradhan,

At Coding Rippler, we’ve seen how AI isn’t just improving existing products but creating completely new opportunities. It’s exciting to help businesses build solutions that customers didn’t even know they needed and then watch those ideas turn into whole new markets!
comments.post_body:
The most exciting AI products aren’t fighting for market share. They’re creating new markets.
When you’re solving expressed needs, you’re usually entering an existing category — trying to do it cheaper, faster, or better.
But when you’re solving latent needs, you often end up creating a category that didn’t exist before.

Think about it:
Users didn’t ask for ride-hailing apps. They asked for “more reliable autos.” The category of on-demand mobility was born.
Nobody asked for ChatGPT. They asked for “better search” or “faster writing.” A new category of AI copilots was born.

That’s the unique power of AI products.
They don’t just compete in markets — they can redefine what a market is.
For Product Managers, this means the questions shift from:
❌ “How do we beat competitors?”
✅ “How do we shape how people think about this problem in the first place?”
Because creating a category isn’t just about technology.
It’s about storytelling, education, and building trust for something users never thought they needed.
And that’s the real privilege of building in AI today:
Not just making products better — but making new possibilities real.

#AIProductManagement #CategoryCreation #LatentNeeds #ProductLeadership

comments.comment_body: Congratulations Sonali Jain
comments.post_body:
There’s always a first time. 🙂
And every first brings its own emotions, excitement, energy, mistakes—and most importantly, learning.

Last week was full of firsts for me:
- My first time at the Microsoft offices in Seattle and the Bay Area
- My first global collaboration across Microsoft teams in three geographies
- My first time working with the investor ecosystem across India and the US in a single format
- And my first time being away from my 3.5-year-old daughter for 8–9 days💙

The highlight of the week: hosting a set of Indian VCs in our Bay Area office and discussing the future of tech and AI. Here are some of my personal reflections:

1. Shift in Microsoft’s DNA
Traditionally, Microsoft has been enterprise-first. But there’s a clear wave building - startups and investors are increasingly shaping how markets evolve and even how we build our products. The startup lens is no longer optional; it’s becoming core.

2. Investors as catalysts for AI in India
Interacting with leaders like Prayank Swaroop, Girish Mathrubootham (yes, a true fan-girl moment!), Lakshmi Shankar, Preeti S., Rahul Agarwalla, Brian Mullen, Krishna M., and Madhur Makkar reinforced a belief: investors are not just funding innovation, they are actively unlocking collaboration. For instance, many are thinking deeply about how their portfolio startups can leverage Microsoft’s distribution, GTM, and global reach.

3. Bridging ecosystems is hard but high-impact
One personal takeaway: as I double down on my role, I see how critical it is to make Microsoft easier to navigate for our investor and startup friends. The opportunity (and responsibility) is to simplify access, accelerate collaboration, and translate our vast ecosystem into tangible outcomes. Matti Suokko shared great learnings on this!

4. Global collaboration is powerful
Bringing together Microsoft's CoreAI leaders in the US with top Indian VCs created conversations that were candid, future-looking, and deeply energizing - from AI infrastructure to agents to scale-up stories. These interactions made it clear that innovation thrives when ecosystems cross borders.

This “first” is definitely one of many more to come. Excited to see how the future unlocks! 🚀

Puneet Chandok Nitin Mittal Kapil Chawla Lindsay Folk Mike Gaal Shaloo Garg Ayelet Steinitz William Tschumy Toni Hasson Berk Smita Roy Annie Pearl Himani Agrawal Micah Baldwin Shanoop Krishnan Rajat Malhotra Aashita Wason Nabyendu M. Vikram Upadhye Nidhi Hasija

I'm just back from our onsite with the whole Gorgias and it was one of the best weeks of my life! 

We celebrated the ten years of the company, it's been a hell of a ride growing the company from 0 to 16,000 brands with Alex Plugaru and the team. 

Now, we’re stepping into the next chapter. 

➡️ We believe conversations will become the primary way people shop. 

Conversational Commerce represents a market 10x larger than customer support software, and merchants are asking for one AI agent that can sell, support, and re-engage their customers seamlessly.

That’s what we’re building today:
- An AI agent that delivers quality, on-brand conversations.
- A seamless shopping experience where chat isn’t just for help, but for buying.
- Smart re-engagement that feels personal, not like another blast.
- An enterprise-ready platform that scales from one agent to thousands.

So, here’s to the next decade! 

If you'd like to join our journey, hit me up on email. If you're a Gorgias customer, I'm curious to get your feedback on these next steps. Thx!

comments.comment_body:
Totally agree! In startups, things are always moving fast and it can feel messy. 

At CodingRippler , we help startups do just that automating workflows and building smart solutions so teams can focus on moving fast with confidence!
comments.post_body:
One thing I’ve learned in nearly a decade of working with startups: No one is handing you a playbook (and if they do, it's typically a draft that's meant to be tweaked, tested, and rewritten as you go)

In a startup, things move fast. Priorities can change in an instant, and structures don’t yet exist. Sometimes you’re building the plane while flying it (actually, it always feels that way 🙃).

And that means self-direction and motivation aren’t just nice to have, they’re a necessity of survival.

But here’s the truth, people don’t always talk about:
- Sometimes you feel alone.
- Sometimes you feel lost.
- Sometimes you wonder if what you’re doing is moving your company in the right direction.

That’s normal.

Self-direction doesn’t mean having all the answers. It means:
- Trusting yourself enough to keep moving forward.
- Backing your gut with data, not just opinions.
- Creating clarity when no one else has it yet.
- Taking action, even in the gray areas.

Startups can sometimes feel messy and isolating. But if you can find your footing in that ambiguity, rely on your team, and try to find confidence in the fact that everyone is in the same boat, that’s when the magic happens! Just keep moving forward, testing, retesting... If it were easy, everyone would do it!

#startups #techstartups #innovation

comments.comment_body: Totally agree – real experience and trusting your own gut beats any startup guide
comments.post_body:
Conventional wisdom in startups comes with a playbook: raise fast, scale faster, follow the formula. Those formulas are more distracting than helpful.

I had an executive coach once tell me to stop reading. It was killing my intuition. I was consuming every startup guide and framework available, trying to find the "right" way to build companies. The problem was all that input was drowning out my intuition and making me second-guess decisions that felt obviously correct.

Some founders don’t like to admit it, but: there is no playbook for the company you’re building. It’s never existed before. 

No book or blog post has seen your customers, your market, or your team. Following someone else's formula means ignoring what your business actually needs. 

Repeat founders have something books can't teach: pattern recognition from real experience. I've seen what works and what doesn't across different companies, markets, and teams. That intuition beats any framework written by someone who's never faced your specific challenges.

comments.comment_body: Great insights! Choosing the right way to test LLMs can make or break AI success
comments.post_body:
85% of AI projects fail to deliver value. 

Air Canada learned this expensively when their chatbot gave incorrect information, leading to tribunal rulings and legal liability. 

Most companies are evaluating LLMs like databases when they should be evaluating them like adaptive biological systems. 

As someone who's built neural control systems for stroke recovery and cancer patient triage, I see the same evaluation mistakes happening across industries. 

I just published a deep dive on why traditional "accuracy testing" fails for LLMs and how to evaluate them properly [linked below]. 

Are you deploying LLMs in your organization? The evaluation framework you choose today determines whether you'll join the 85% that fail or the 15% that succeed. 

﻿#AIStrategy #EnterpriseAI #RiskManagement
